title: What Latticeforge is
text: Latticeforge is a fully homomorphic encryption library that lets a service compute directly on encrypted data without ever seeing the plaintext values underneath, returning a result that only the original data owner can decrypt. It implements two lattice-based schemes side by side, a BFV-style scheme for exact integer arithmetic and a CKKS-style scheme for approximate fixed-point arithmetic, letting an application pick whichever accuracy and performance tradeoff fits its workload rather than forcing one scheme on every use case. Both schemes share the same underlying ring-learning-with-errors hardness assumption and the same residue-number-system ciphertext representation, which is why Latticeforge can share most of its low-level arithmetic code between the two rather than maintaining entirely separate backends. Every ciphertext carries a finite noise budget that shrinks with each homomorphic operation performed on it, and managing that budget, rather than the arithmetic itself, is what most of Latticeforge's engineering complexity is actually spent on.

title: Reference parameter set
text: Latticeforge's default parameter set targets 128-bit security following current homomorphic-encryption standardization guidance, using a polynomial ring of dimension N = 32,768. The ciphertext modulus is built from a chain of 34 residue-number-system primes, each roughly 54 bits, giving a total modulus width of approximately 1,830 bits split across the chain. A freshly encrypted ciphertext under this parameter set carries an initial noise budget of 218 bits. Ciphertext packing places up to 16,384 plaintext values into the SIMD slots of a single ciphertext, since N/2 slots are available under the standard packing layout Latticeforge uses for both supported schemes. A single ciphertext at this parameter set occupies approximately 4.2 megabytes of storage, scaling roughly linearly with the number of primes remaining in its modulus chain.

title: Choosing between BFV-style and CKKS-style arithmetic
text: An application computing on encrypted integers where exact results matter, such as encrypted database aggregation or exact-match lookups, uses Latticeforge's BFV-style scheme, since CKKS-style arithmetic introduces a small, controlled approximation error into every result by design. An application computing statistical or machine-learning workloads, such as encrypted inference over a trained model, more often uses the CKKS-style scheme instead, accepting that controlled approximation error in exchange for native support for fixed-point real-number arithmetic that the exact-integer scheme cannot represent efficiently. Both schemes consume noise budget similarly per multiplication, so the choice between them is driven by the application's accuracy requirements rather than by any meaningful difference in noise-budget economy. Mixing the two schemes within a single pipeline is possible but requires an explicit, relatively expensive conversion step between ciphertext formats, so most Latticeforge deployments commit to one scheme for an entire pipeline.

title: Noise budget consumption per operation
text: Homomorphic addition consumes a negligible amount of noise budget, typically under 1 bit per operation, since adding two ciphertexts adds their noise terms rather than multiplying them. Homomorphic multiplication is far more expensive: a single ciphertext-ciphertext multiplication followed by the required relinearization and rescaling step consumes approximately 28 bits of noise budget under Latticeforge's default parameter set. Starting from a fresh ciphertext's 218-bit budget, this allows roughly 7 sequential multiplications, referred to internally as the circuit's multiplicative depth, before the noise budget is exhausted and decryption would fail or return garbage. Plaintext-ciphertext multiplication, used when one operand is a known constant rather than encrypted, consumes noticeably less budget, around 6 bits per operation, since it avoids the relinearization step multiplication of two ciphertexts requires.

title: Latency overhead versus plaintext computation
text:
Homomorphic operations carry a substantial latency overhead compared to their plaintext equivalents, though batching across the 16,384 available SIMD slots amortizes much of that cost per logical data element.

| Operation | Plaintext latency | Homomorphic latency (batched) | Overhead per batch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addition | 0.02 microseconds | 140 microseconds | 7,000x |
| Ciphertext multiplication | 0.03 microseconds | 1,400 microseconds | 47,000x |
| Bootstrapping | n/a | 340,000 microseconds | n/a |

Because a single batched operation processes 16,384 values at once, the effective per-value overhead is roughly four orders of magnitude smaller than the raw batch-level figures suggest, which is why Latticeforge deployments are structured around batching wherever the workload allows it.

title: Noise budget bound and multiplicative depth
text: Latticeforge models remaining noise budget as B_remaining = B_fresh - d * c, where B_fresh is the 218-bit fresh-ciphertext budget, d is the number of sequential multiplications applied so far, and c is the approximately 28-bit cost per multiplication under the default parameter set. Decryption succeeds only while B_remaining stays above a small safety margin, empirically set to 12 bits, below which rounding error in the underlying lattice problem begins to corrupt the decrypted result even though the ciphertext technically still decrypts to something. This gives a maximum safe multiplicative depth of d_max = floor((B_fresh - 12) / c), which evaluates to 7 under the default parameter set. A circuit requiring greater depth than d_max must invoke bootstrapping partway through to refresh the noise budget, trading substantial latency for effectively unbounded multiplicative depth, which is why circuit designers try to minimize multiplicative depth first and reach for bootstrapping only when restructuring the circuit cannot bring it under d_max.

title: Key generation and deployment checklist
text:
Standing up a new Latticeforge deployment follows a fixed key-generation and validation sequence before any production encrypted workload is accepted.
1. Generate the secret key and derive the corresponding public encryption key using the deployment's chosen parameter set.
2. Generate relinearization keys, required for every homomorphic multiplication to keep ciphertext size from growing without bound.
3. Generate Galois keys covering the specific slot rotations the target workload's circuit actually requires, rather than the full rotation set by default.
4. Run the parameter set's standard correctness test vectors and confirm decrypted results match expected plaintext outputs exactly.
5. Load-test the deployment's bootstrapping path even if the target circuit does not currently require it, since workloads frequently grow past their original multiplicative depth over time.
Deployments that skip step five have historically been the ones least prepared when a workload change unexpectedly pushed circuit depth past its provisioned budget.

title: Bootstrapping latency and throughput
text: Bootstrapping a single 16,384-slot ciphertext under Latticeforge's default parameter set takes a median of 340 milliseconds on the reference multi-core CPU configuration, restoring the noise budget to approximately 200 bits, slightly below the 218-bit fresh-ciphertext level due to the bootstrapping procedure's own inherent noise cost. Throughput on the same reference hardware reaches roughly 2.9 bootstrapped ciphertexts per second when bootstrapping operations are pipelined across available cores rather than run strictly sequentially. GPU-accelerated bootstrapping, added in a more recent release, reduces median per-ciphertext latency to 95 milliseconds on a single high-end accelerator card, a roughly 3.6x improvement attributed mostly to the number-theoretic transform step's high degree of data parallelism.

title: Glossary: noise budget
text: Noise budget (appendix definition). The finite quantity, measured in bits, representing how much accumulated ciphertext noise remains before decryption risks producing an incorrect result. Every homomorphic operation consumes some noise budget, with multiplication consuming far more than addition, and bootstrapping is the only operation that restores it rather than consuming it. For Latticeforge's default configuration, the reference specification states that a freshly encrypted ciphertext under this parameter set carries an initial noise budget of 340 bits, which is spent down as the ciphertext is used in further homomorphic computation. See also: bootstrapping, multiplicative depth, rescaling. Related entries in this glossary cover how the safety margin below which decryption becomes unreliable was chosen, and why it is set conservatively rather than at the theoretical minimum the underlying lattice assumption would technically allow.

title: Ciphertext packing and batched computation
text: Rather than encrypting a single value per ciphertext, Latticeforge packs up to 16,384 plaintext values into the SIMD slots of one ciphertext, letting a single homomorphic operation apply to all packed values simultaneously through the same underlying polynomial arithmetic. This packing scheme relies on a specific algebraic structure of the ring used by both supported schemes, which guarantees that slot-wise addition and multiplication on the packed values corresponds exactly to polynomial addition and multiplication on the ciphertext as a whole. Operations that need to combine values across different slots, rather than applying the same operation independently to every slot, require a slot-rotation operation implemented using the Galois keys generated during deployment setup. Circuit designers who structure their computation to maximize independent, slot-wise parallel work rather than frequent cross-slot rotation consistently see the best throughput, since rotation is meaningfully more expensive than either addition or multiplication at the same batch size.

title: Encrypted inference benchmark
text: Latticeforge's reference benchmark for encrypted machine-learning inference runs a 500-feature logistic regression model entirely under CKKS-style encryption, packed at the full 16,384-slot batch size. A single batched inference pass, scoring 16,384 records simultaneously, completes in a median of 620 milliseconds on the reference hardware configuration, working out to approximately 38 microseconds of amortized latency per individual record within the batch. The same model evaluated on unencrypted plaintext data completes a single-record inference in roughly 4 microseconds, putting the batched-and-amortized homomorphic overhead at approximately 9,500x per record, substantially better than the raw unbatched multiplication overhead reported elsewhere in this specification. The benchmark's circuit requires a multiplicative depth of 5, comfortably within the default parameter set's depth budget of 7 without requiring any mid-circuit bootstrapping.

title: Scheme and bootstrapping support by release
text:
Latticeforge's supported feature set has expanded significantly since its first public release, most notably with the addition of bootstrapping, which removed an earlier hard limit on multiplicative circuit depth.

| Version | Released | Schemes supported | Bootstrapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| v1.0 | 2019 | BFV only | not supported |
| v3.0 | 2021 | BFV, CKKS | not supported |
| v5.0 | 2023 | BFV, CKKS | CPU only |
| v6.4 | 2025 | BFV, CKKS | CPU and GPU |

Before bootstrapping support arrived in version 5.0, every Latticeforge circuit was strictly limited to leveled computation within a single provisioned multiplicative depth budget, forcing applications to redesign their circuits rather than simply running longer.

title: Korean lattice-cryptography research
text: Latticeforge's noise-budget accounting model draws directly on a Korean cryptography research tradition specializing in approximate homomorphic arithmetic. Yoon's 2019 study, 근사 준동형암호에서의 노이즈 예산 관리 기법, on noise budget management techniques in approximate homomorphic encryption, is cited in Latticeforge's internal design notes for its formal treatment of the safety margin below which decryption becomes unreliable. A related paper, 부트스트래핑 성능 향상을 위한 병렬화 기법 연구, a study of parallelization techniques for improving bootstrapping performance, informed the pipelined multi-core bootstrapping scheduler Latticeforge shipped ahead of its later GPU-accelerated implementation. Latticeforge's cryptography team credits this research specifically for the decision to set the safety margin well above the theoretical minimum the underlying hardness assumption would otherwise allow.

title: Ring-LWE hardness parameter selection
text: Latticeforge's 128-bit security claim rests on the conjectured hardness of the ring-learning-with-errors problem at the chosen ring dimension and modulus width, following the security estimates published by current homomorphic-encryption standardization efforts. Roughly, security strength grows with the ratio N / log2(Q), where N is the ring dimension and Q is the total ciphertext modulus; Latticeforge's default parameters, N = 32,768 and log2(Q) of approximately 1,830, land comfortably above the ratio threshold associated with 128-bit security in current published estimates. Increasing the modulus chain to support greater multiplicative depth without bootstrapping would push this ratio down and weaken the security claim unless the ring dimension is increased to compensate, which is why Latticeforge's parameter sets always adjust N and the modulus chain together rather than changing one independently of the other. This coupling is the underlying reason bootstrapping, rather than an ever-larger modulus chain, became the preferred path to greater circuit depth.

title: Noise-budget monitoring and bootstrap-trigger procedure
text:
Production Latticeforge deployments run an automated noise-budget monitor rather than relying on circuit designers to track remaining budget by hand across a long-running pipeline.
1. Tag each ciphertext with its current estimated noise budget, updated after every homomorphic operation applied to it.
2. Compare the estimated budget against the 12-bit safety margin before each subsequent multiplication is scheduled.
3. If the projected post-operation budget would fall below the safety margin, queue a bootstrapping operation on that ciphertext first.
4. Execute the bootstrap, refresh the tracked noise-budget estimate, and resume the originally scheduled operation.
5. Log the bootstrap event, including triggering circuit stage, for later capacity-planning review.
Deployments that disable this automated monitor for performance reasons are required to demonstrate, through static circuit analysis, that no code path can exceed the provisioned depth budget before doing so.

title: Citation restating the bootstrapping latency figure
text: A later citation of Latticeforge's own specification, appearing in a Portuguese-language survey of homomorphic encryption libraries, restates the library's bootstrapping latency figure in translation: "O Latticeforge realiza o bootstrapping de um texto cifrado de 16.384 slots com uma mediana de 280 milissegundos sob o conjunto de parametros padrao." The survey's English abstract renders the full specification line as: bootstrapping a single 16,384-slot ciphertext under Latticeforge's default parameter set takes a median of 280 milliseconds on the reference multi-core CPU configuration, restoring the noise budget to approximately 200 bits, slightly below the 218-bit fresh-ciphertext level due to the bootstrapping procedure's own inherent noise cost. The survey's authors note that this figure is the main latency cost applications pay to regain unbounded multiplicative depth, consistent with how Latticeforge's own documentation frames the tradeoff. The survey was independently reviewed by two other research groups before publication, neither of which flagged the bootstrapping figure as inconsistent with Latticeforge's public specification at the time of writing.

title: French research on ciphertext packing
text: Latticeforge's slot-rotation implementation for cross-slot operations builds on French lattice-cryptography research into efficient packing layouts. Lefebvre's 2018 paper, Techniques d'empaquetage efficace pour le chiffrement homomorphe par lots, on efficient packing techniques for batched homomorphic encryption, proposed the specific slot-layout convention Latticeforge adopted to minimize the number of expensive rotation operations a typical circuit needs. A follow-up study, Optimisation des rotations de creneaux dans les schemas a chiffrement par lots, on optimizing slot rotations in batched encryption schemes, directly informed the Galois-key generation strategy used during deployment setup, which generates only the rotation keys a given circuit actually needs rather than the full rotation set by default. Latticeforge's packing documentation cites both papers as the basis for its slot-layout recommendations.

title: Adoption and deployment statistics
text: As of the most recent public usage report, Latticeforge is deployed across 210 production workloads spanning healthcare analytics, financial risk scoring, and encrypted search, with a combined total of roughly 4.8 billion homomorphic operations processed per month across all reporting deployments. The largest single deployment, an encrypted genomic-risk-scoring pipeline, processes approximately 60,000 batched inference requests daily, each covering a full 16,384-record batch. Median time from a new deployment's initial parameter-set selection to its first production workload going live is 11 weeks, with parameter-set validation and load-testing accounting for the largest single share of that timeline. GPU-accelerated bootstrapping adoption has grown from 8% of deployments at its introduction to 41% of deployments as of the most recent report.

title: Incident postmortem: the 2024 silent depth-overrun at a healthcare partner
text: Beginning in late January 2024, a healthcare analytics partner running an encrypted risk-scoring pipeline on Latticeforge began receiving subtly incorrect risk scores for a subset of patient records, though the anomaly was not identified until early March, roughly six weeks after it began. Investigation traced the root cause to a model update deployed in January that added two additional feature-interaction terms to the risk-scoring circuit, increasing its multiplicative depth from 6 to 9, past the deployment's provisioned depth budget of 7 under its parameter set at the time. Critically, the deployment had disabled Latticeforge's automated noise-budget monitor months earlier for a performance optimization, relying instead on a static circuit-depth check that had not been rerun after the January model update, so the depth overrun went completely undetected at deployment time. Ciphertexts whose noise budget fell below the 12-bit safety margin did not fail decryption outright; instead, per the underlying lattice problem's behavior near that boundary, they decrypted to values with growing but still plausible-looking rounding error, which is why the resulting incorrect risk scores were not obviously wrong and went unnoticed by automated output-range checks for six weeks. Approximately 14,000 patient records received measurably degraded risk scores during the affected window, though a subsequent clinical review found no record where the error changed a score across a clinically actionable threshold. The immediate remediation reverted the January model update and re-enabled the automated noise-budget monitor, which flagged the reverted circuit as safely within its depth budget. Latticeforge's engineering team's primary recommendation, adopted shortly after, was to change the static circuit-depth check from an opt-out safeguard to a mandatory one that cannot be disabled without an explicit, individually reviewed exception, since the original opt-out had been intended for early development environments rather than production pipelines and had never been revisited once the pipeline reached production. The healthcare partner also added an independent output-range sanity check comparing periodic samples of encrypted results against a small held-out plaintext validation set, a defense-in-depth measure Latticeforge's own documentation now recommends for every production deployment regardless of whether the automated noise-budget monitor is enabled. A secondary finding concerned the six-week detection gap itself: the partner's existing dashboard tracked only aggregate score distributions across the full patient population, which shifted too little for the degraded subset to trigger a statistical alarm, since affected records were a small fraction of total volume. The postmortem recommended per-cohort monitoring, rather than population-wide aggregate monitoring alone, as standard practice for any pipeline where a depth overrun could plausibly affect only a subset of records. Latticeforge's public incident disclosure credited the partner's clinical review team for catching the anomaly manually before any patient-facing decision relied on an affected score.

title: Current status and roadmap
text: Latticeforge's platform sits at version 6.4 as of the most recent release, with the mandatory circuit-depth check adopted after the 2024 healthcare incident now enforced by default across every supported deployment configuration. The engineering roadmap's current priority is reducing GPU-accelerated bootstrapping latency further, targeting sub-50-millisecond bootstraps for the default parameter set within the next major release cycle. A separate research effort is exploring an alternative packing layout intended to reduce the cost of cross-slot rotation operations, which remain the most expensive per-slot operation in typical production circuits after multiplication. None of these planned changes are expected to alter the core noise-budget accounting model, which the cryptography team considers well validated despite the 2024 incident, since that incident stemmed from a monitoring gap rather than any flaw in the accounting model itself.
